identity.mission: REDACTED exists to investigate the documented, declassified, and court-proven operations that governments, corporations, and institutions deliberately hid from the public. The channel works from primary sources only — FOIA releases, congressional hearings, leaked cables, court depositions, internal memos, whistleblower testimony, and declassified intelligence files. The goal is to show viewers the gap between the official story and what the documents actually say. Every episode answers one question: what did they know, when did they know it, and who paid the price for the silence?
identity.audience: REDACTED is built for skeptical, intelligent viewers between 22 and 50 who feel something is off about the way the world is presented to them, but who reject lazy speculation, baseless theories, and tribal politics. The audience is drawn to investigative journalism, declassified history, intelligence operations, corporate corruption, regulatory capture, whistleblower stories, and the slow careful work of uncovering documented truth. They watch Adam Curtis, Frontline, Errol Morris, and read Glenn Greenwald, Seymour Hersh, and Jane Mayer. They want to understand how power actually works, not be told a comforting story about it.
identity.forbidden: election fraud claims, anti-vaccine narratives, Sandy Hook or mass shooting denial, QAnon, flat earth, chemtrails, lizard people, current-events partisan politics, religious conspiracy, antisemitic tropes, anti-LGBT framings, anything not supported by primary sources
identity.refs: Adam Curtis, Frontline, Errol Morris, Vice News, Retro Report, Real Stories, Seymour Hersh, Jane Mayer, Glenn Greenwald

script.tone:
The writing tone should be that of an investigative journalist building a case file. Cold. Patient. Evidentiary. Every sentence is a piece of evidence being placed on the table for the viewer to examine.

The narration reads like a long-form magazine article — The New Yorker, The Atlantic, Harper's — adapted for the screen. Sentences are measured. Claims are sourced. Speculation is labeled as speculation. The viewer should never feel they are being told what to think, only what the record shows.

The writing should feel forensic. Like FOIA documents being read aloud. Like a deposition transcript narrated by someone who has read every page twice.

Key tonal characteristics:

Cold and measured rather than urgent or breathless
Forensic precision — names, dates, document numbers, file codes when they exist
The word 'allegedly' is sacred. So is 'according to' and 'the documents show'
Never describe a person as evil, corrupt, or villainous — let the evidence describe them
Never editorialize emotion — describe the action, the document, the testimony, and let the viewer feel the weight
Use paragraph-length pauses where the viewer needs to absorb a fact
The most powerful sentences are the shortest ones
Quote document language verbatim when possible, in italics, with citation

The script structure mirrors investigative journalism:

The official story (what we were told)
The gap (something doesn't add up)
The documents (what the record shows)
The operation (what actually happened, step by step)
The cost (who was harmed)
The cover-up (how it was hidden)
The disclosure (how it came out — FOIA year, congressional hearing, leak)
The aftermath (who was held accountable, who wasn't)
The lesson (what this tells us about how power operates)

Every script must:

Name at least three primary source documents by name and date
Name the people who declassified, leaked, or testified
Distinguish proven facts from open questions
Acknowledge what the documents do NOT prove
Avoid the words 'they' and 'them' as shorthand for institutions — be specific about which agency, which administration, which executives
Avoid suggesting motive when motive is not documented
End with a single restrained observation, not a call to action

The tone should never be:

Breathless
Sensational
Finger-pointing
Moralizing
Sarcastic
Mocking
Gleeful about the corruption uncovered
Certain about things that are uncertain
script.hook: Start with one sentence from a declassified document, read in a flat voice, with the document itself filling the screen. No music. No graphics. Just the document and the voice. Then a 1.5 second pause. Then the operation name and the year. Example: '"The Agency has determined that the subjects will not be informed of the nature of the testing." — MKUltra Subproject 68, internal memo, March 1955.' Hold. Title card: MKULTRA. 1953 to 1973.
script.subjects: MKUltra, Operation Northwoods, COINTELPRO, Iran-Contra, Operation Mockingbird, Operation Paperclip, Tuskegee Syphilis Study, Project SHAMROCK, Operation CHAOS, Operation Ajax, Operation PBSUCCESS, Bay of Pigs internal record, Pentagon Papers, Watergate, Family Jewels documents, Church Committee findings, Pike Committee findings, Iran-Contra Tower Commission, Snowden disclosures, Vault 7, NSA bulk collection, Stellar Wind, Stingray devices, Tobacco Master Settlement documents, Purdue Pharma internal emails, OxyContin marketing files, asbestos industry coverups, lead industry coverups, leaded gasoline internal Ethyl Corporation records, ExxonMobil climate research 1977-1989, DuPont C8 internal records, Flint water crisis internal emails, Boeing 737 MAX MCAS internal communications, Theranos SEC filings, Volkswagen dieselgate internal engineering memos, Cambridge Analytica whistleblower testimony, Panama Papers, Pandora Papers, FinCEN files, FIFA corruption indictments, FIFA Garcia Report, Catholic Church John Jay Report, Boy Scouts perversion files
script.lesson: Every episode ends with a single observation grounded in the documents — not a call to action, not a moral. Examples: 'The program was renamed twice. It was never ended.' / 'Three of the executives named in the deposition still sit on corporate boards.' / 'The class action settled for eighty-six million dollars. Internal documents value the harm at three point two billion.' The viewer is left with a fact, not a feeling.
script.forbidden_words: shocking, you won't believe, the truth they don't want, wake up, they, them (when used as a vague stand-in for institutions), evil, sinister, mind-blowing, exposed (overused), nobody is talking about, the elite, the cabal, the establishment (as a vague noun), false flag
script.power_words: declassified, redacted, deposition, memo, hearing, document, testimony, internal, FOIA, subpoenaed, indictment, settlement, whistleblower, evidence, record, transcript, exhibit, finding, ruling, disclosure, leaked, classified, marked, dated, signed, authorized, approved, denied, withheld, omitted

visuals.vis_palette: Black, deep red (#a01010 to #ff3333 accent only), bone white, manila folder tan, federal-document beige, redaction-bar black
visuals.vis_accent_color: Red used sparingly — only for redaction bars, classification stamps, blood-in-evidence moments, and document highlights. White for document text. Manila for folder textures. Yellow is forbidden — too tabloid.
visuals.vis_broll_rules:
* document → close-up of declassified page with FOIA stamp, manila folder being opened, fingers turning a page, redaction bar being painted over text in slow motion
* hearing → C-SPAN archival footage, congressional chamber, witness chair, microphone close-up, gavel, water glass on green felt table
* deposition → court reporter stenograph, transcript pages, sworn-in hand on document
* surveillance → CCTV grain, infrared, telephoto through window blinds, parabolic dish, telephone pole junction box, wiretap reel-to-reel
* intelligence → CIA seal at Langley, NSA Fort Meade aerial, FBI Hoover building, glass-walled conference room, classified safe being opened, briefcase handcuffed to wrist
* whistleblower → silhouette in dim room, voice modulator, walking away from camera, manila envelope being slid across diner table, payphone at night
* leak → USB drive on wood desk, encrypted laptop screen, Signal messaging, dead drop, newspaper printing press at night, journalist reading documents alone
* cover-up → shredder at full speed, paper being burned in metal can, hard drive being drilled, file being marked DESTROYED, archive room emptied
* corporation → glass tower from below, boardroom from outside through window, executive elevator, corporate logo on building, annual report cover, lobbyist walking K Street
* government building → White House dawn, Capitol dome, Pentagon overhead, Foggy Bottom, J. Edgar Hoover building, Langley sign
* archive → microfiche reader, climate-controlled storage, librarian gloves handling document, presidential library, National Archives reading room
* operation in progress → black SUV motorcade, unmarked van, agents in earpieces, hangar at night, tarmac of unnamed airfield, satellite imagery with grid overlay
* harm → empty hospital corridor, headstone with date, family photograph in frame, courtroom from gallery, victim impact statement being read
* time period — Cold War → Eisenhower-era newsreel, black and white press conference, IBM punch cards, switchboard operators, mid-century federal architecture
* time period — 70s → grainy 16mm, Church Committee hearings, Carter-era press room, IBM Selectric typewriter, wood-paneled office
* time period — 80s → Reagan-era TV news, Iran-Contra hearings, fax machine, early CRT terminals
* time period — 90s/2000s → handycam DV, courthouse steps press scrum, early laptops, cable news lower thirds, satellite truck
* time period — 2010s/present → drone shot of corporate campus, smartphone screen, server farm interior, encrypted-messaging interface
* money → cash bands on table, shell company filing, offshore account ledger, wire transfer screen, Swiss bank vault door, Cayman Islands aerial
* power → handshake in hallway, signature on document, presidential pen ceremony, swearing-in, lobbyist business card, revolving door with name plate
* secrecy → SCIF door closing, classified stamp coming down, document marked TOP SECRET, eyes-only envelope, vault tumbler
* the press → typewriter keys, newsroom hum, front page being laid out, journalist on phone with notepad, microfilm of old reportage, Pulitzer announcement
* victims → empty playground, abandoned town, plaintiff photo at press conference, memorial wall, candle vigil, courtroom victim statement
* aftermath → empty chair where defendant sat, headlines stacking, settlement check, congressional report being released, library shelf of investigative books on the subject
visuals.vis_forbidden:
* No fake leaked footage — only real archival or clearly labeled b-roll
* No reenactments with actors playing real people
* No green-screen 'agents in suits' stock footage
* No CGI explosions, fake helicopter shots, or action-movie aesthetics
* No Anonymous masks, hooded hackers at green-text terminals, or 'mr robot' clichés
* No spinning newspaper transitions
* No conspiracy-board red string montages (overused and cheap)
* No lizard-people / Illuminati / occult symbols
* No dramatic zoom on the eye of a dollar bill or any pyramid imagery
* No 'shadowy figure' silhouette except when literally a whistleblower being protected
* No dramatic music spikes on every revelation — let silence carry weight
* No fake document close-ups with lorem ipsum or fictional letterheads — use real declassified documents
* No grainy footage for the sake of looking spooky — grain should match the era
* No 'WAKE UP' style on-screen text
* No yellow journalism color palette (tabloid red and yellow)
* No flashing 'CLASSIFIED' watermarks on every cut
* No countdown timers unless documenting an actual countdown event
* No conspiratorial 'connect the dots' lines drawn across photos unless the documents themselves do so
* No images of current-events political figures unless they are direct subjects of declassified material
* No suggesting motive through visual juxtaposition that the documents do not support
* Every visual should: support a sourced claim, illustrate a documented fact, establish a time period, or convey the human cost — never suggest something the documents do not say
visuals.vis_aroll_rule: There is no celebrity A-roll. The voice is a constant narrator. The visual A-roll equivalent is the document — declassified pages, hearing transcripts, internal memos. The document fills the frame for emphasis the way a celebrity face would in CH1. Hold on a document for 4–6 seconds when the narration quotes from it directly. Use a slow Ken-Burns zoom on the relevant paragraph. Highlight the cited line in subtle yellow underline only when the voice reads it.

mograph.triggers:
* classified → black document on table, classification stamp slams down in slow motion (TOP SECRET / EYES ONLY / CONFIDENTIAL) with red ink bleeding into the paper, subtle paper rustle sound
* redacted → manila document page slowly scrolling, redaction bars appearing one by one over names and locations, sound of marker on paper
* operation → world map with single red pin dropping at the geographic location of the operation, name and date appearing below in typewriter font
* timeline → horizontal timeline appearing on dark background, single point of light traveling along it, key dates appearing as small typewriter labels as the light passes
* memo → close-up of a typewritten internal memo, camera slowly tilting down the page, key sentence highlighted in subtle yellow underline as it scrolls
* deposition → court transcript being printed line by line by an unseen stenograph, voice of testimony fading in over typing sound
* hearing → congressional hearing chamber in greyscale, slow zoom toward the witness chair, name plate of witness illuminating last
* leak → single envelope sliding into frame on dark surface, contents spilling out as photographs, the photographs scattering and arranging into a pattern
* shredder → industrial shredder consuming a stack of documents, paper strips falling like snow against black background, single intact page floating down on top of pile
* surveillance → ceiling tile lifting to reveal small camera lens, slow zoom into the lens until the iris fills frame
* wiretap → close-up of reel-to-reel tape rotating, sound of recorded voice playing backwards faintly, then forwards revealing a single word
* witness → empty wooden chair under single overhead light, slow camera arc around the chair, the chair remains empty — implying a witness who never testified
* cover-up → archive room full of file cabinets, drawers opening on their own, files flying out and disintegrating mid-air
* exposure → newspaper front page printing on press at speed, headline coming into focus as the press slows, ink still wet
* settlement → courthouse exterior at dusk, plaintiff and defendant exiting separate doors, the camera holds on the empty space between them
* indictment → gavel coming down on grand jury paperwork, the strike echoing in an empty chamber
* compartmentalization → diagram of organizational chart with single node lighting up while others dim, lines connecting it broken or missing
* dead drop → park bench at night, fallen leaves, single envelope wedged into the slats, footsteps approaching and leaving
* whistleblower → silhouette walking through an underground parking garage, footsteps echoing, single column of light from above
* archive vault → vault door swinging open in slow motion, fluorescent lights flickering on row by row revealing endless shelves of boxes
* FOIA → standard federal FOIA request form filling out itself in real time, the words REQUEST GRANTED stamping across it after long delay, date showing years of bureaucratic wait
* pattern → multiple identical documents appearing on a dark surface, arranging themselves into a grid, common phrases highlighting across all of them simultaneously
* timeline gap → calendar pages flipping rapidly forward, then suddenly stopping on a blank page — the missing years of an operation
* organizational chart → corporate or agency hierarchy diagram, key names redacting one at a time as the camera holds
* destruction order → handwritten note reading DESTROY appearing on screen as if written by an unseen hand, the note then being itself destroyed
* the gap → side-by-side comparison of two documents — the public statement on the left, the internal memo on the right — both highlighting in opposite colors at the contradiction
* aftermath → empty congressional chamber after a hearing, papers still on desks, slow pan across abandoned seats
* statute of limitations → calendar showing dates struck through, final date with stamp reading TIME EXPIRED
* sealed → folder with red seal being applied, seal cracking only slightly to suggest future disclosure
mograph.style:
* Motion graphics should look like declassified document forensics — like the viewer is sitting inside a National Archives reading room
* Texture is paramount: every document should feel scanned, photocopied, faxed, or microfilmed — not crisp and digital
* Use real document fonts: IBM Selectric typewriter, Courier, Times New Roman 11pt, hand-stamped classification marks
* Color is restrained: black, manila, bone white, federal-record red for stamps only
* Motion should be slow and deliberate — documents move like they are being physically placed and read
* Use organic micro-movements: paper edges curling slightly, dust particles in the lamp light, slight typewriter shake on each character
* Avoid all sleek modern motion design — no smooth gradient transitions, no neon glows, no minimalist sans-serif elegance
* Avoid corporate explainer aesthetic completely — this is not a TED talk graphic
* Avoid hacker-movie aesthetic — no green terminal text scrolling, no Matrix code, no fake terminal UI
* The redaction bar is the channel's signature motif — use it precisely and only over real surnames, real places, real operation codenames as they would have been redacted in actual FOIA releases
* Stamps, seals, and signatures should always look hand-applied — slightly tilted, slightly uneven ink coverage
* Use typewriter sound design throughout text reveals — keystroke per letter, carriage return at end of paragraph
* The yellow highlighter underline is the second signature motif — used only when narration quotes verbatim from a document, applied during the spoken line, fades after
* For organizational diagrams, use vintage hand-drawn aesthetic — like a Church Committee whiteboard photographed in 1975
* Maps should be vintage atlas style or military-grid style, not Google Maps
* Timelines should look like courtroom exhibits, not infographics
* Photographs of real people should always be sourced archive material — never AI-generated faces, never stock photos meant to look like the subject
* When showing the gap between public statement and internal record, always show both side by side at the same scale, with the same lighting — let the viewer judge
mograph.text_style: Typewriter font. Courier or IBM Selectric. Black on bone-white when over document. White on black for standalone statements. Words appear letter by letter at typewriter speed (~12 characters per second) with audible keystroke. Maximum 7 words. Hold for 3 seconds. Exit by fade or by being stamped over with red CLASSIFIED.

sound.mood:
The sound design should feel like sitting inside a climate-controlled archive at night. Quiet. Hollow. The hum of fluorescent lights. The distant whir of a microfilm reader. The clack of an old typewriter two rooms away.

Music should be sparse, ambient, evidentiary. Not cinematic. Not orchestral. Not motivational. The closest reference points are: the sound design of Errol Morris's The Fog of War, Adam Curtis's HyperNormalisation, Philip Glass's score for The Thin Blue Line, and the early seasons of Serial.

For the first 90 seconds of any video:

* No music
* Only: room tone, distant typewriter, faint paper rustle, the voice
* Let the silence build the weight of the evidence being presented
* Music enters only when the operation itself is named, not before

When music does enter:

* Single sustained low drone, no melody
* Subtle string pad, very low in mix (-18dB)
* No drums, no percussion of any kind for the first half of the video
* No rising swells, no triumphant moments

For the operation section:

* Repetitive minimalist piano figure (Philip Glass / Max Richter style) — single phrase, looped, slowly modulating
* Low cello drone underneath
* Subtle synthesizer pad
* Occasional sub-bass impact only on the heaviest revelations — used maximum 3 times per video

For the cover-up section:

* Music thins out
* Increase room tone
* Bring in: tape hiss, faint static, the sound of an air conditioner in an empty building
* Music drops out entirely when reading verbatim from a document

For the disclosure section:

* The music returns slowly
* Add: a slow rising synth pad, very gradual
* Never let the music become triumphant — disclosure is not victory, it is acknowledgment of what was always there

For the lesson section:

* Single piano note with long sustain
* Hold the silence between notes for 3-5 seconds
* Final 30 seconds: no music at all
* The video ends in room tone — the sound of an empty archive

Avoid completely:

* Cinematic trailer impacts (the 'BWAAA' sound)
* Orchestral swells
* Electronic dance elements
* Hip-hop drum patterns
* Anything that sounds like a true-crime podcast theme
* Tense violin staccato (overused, melodramatic)
* Heartbeat sound effects
* Whoosh transition sounds (use document rustle instead)
* Any sound that signals 'something dramatic is happening' — the documents themselves are the drama
sound.ambient: Air conditioning hum, distant typewriter, fluorescent buzz, paper rustle, microfilm reader whir, courthouse acoustic, archive room reverb
sound.silence: Music drops to total silence whenever the narrator reads verbatim from a document. The document text appears on screen during the silence. Music returns 1 second after the quotation ends. Minimum silent reading per video: 3 instances.
sound.swells: There are no triumphant swells. There is one moment, in the disclosure section, where the bass note deepens by +3dB for 8 seconds — this is the closest the channel comes to a swell. Music ends 30 seconds before the video. The final 30 seconds are room tone only.
sound.voice_fx: clean, slight room reverb (-22dB return) to suggest the narrator is reading in an enclosed space
sound.extra: Typewriter keystroke sound effect (single key, soft) on every appearance of on-screen text. Carriage return sound at the end of every paragraph of text. Stamp impact sound on every classification stamp. Paper rustle on every document scene change.

editing.edit_pacing:
* Cold open (0:00–0:20) = the slowest pacing in the entire video

  * Single document on screen, voice reads one sentence from it
  * Hold for 3-4 seconds in silence after the quote
  * Title card appears: operation name, date range
  * No music yet, no graphics, no movement
  * The viewer should feel like they have just sat down at a table with a file

* Section 1 — The Official Story (20% of runtime) = measured, restrained

  * Average shot length: 6-10 seconds
  * Show archival news footage of the public narrative at the time
  * Show official statements, press conferences, sanitized denials
  * Use cross dissolves between archival clips — they should feel of the same era
  * Begin to introduce the first hints of doubt — the question that doesn't have an answer
  * Pacing remains slow — the viewer should be settled in, listening, like watching a Frontline opening

* Section 2 — The Gap (15% of runtime) = slightly tighter, evidentiary

  * Average shot length: 5-8 seconds
  * Begin showing the contradictions: side-by-side public statement vs internal memo
  * Use the split-screen mograph here
  * Hold on the contradiction for at least 4 seconds — let the viewer read both sides
  * Music remains absent or barely audible

* Section 3 — The Documents (35% of runtime) = the core of the video, controlled rhythm

  * This is the longest section and the spine of every episode
  * Average shot length: 4-7 seconds, varying by document weight
  * Show actual declassified pages, hearing transcripts, internal communications
  * Use Ken-Burns slow zoom on the relevant paragraph
  * Apply yellow underline highlight in sync with narration reading the cited line
  * Use typewriter sound for every text reveal
  * Hold for 3-4 full seconds on each cited paragraph after the narration reads it — let the viewer absorb the source
  * Introduce the minimalist piano underneath, very low in the mix

* Section 4 — The Operation (15% of runtime) = the most kinetic section, but still restrained by channel standards

  * Average shot length: 3-5 seconds
  * Show how the operation unfolded — locations, dates, participants
  * Use mograph: timeline, map pins, organizational chart
  * This is where the music is most present — but still no drums
  * Increase use of period archival footage — newsreel, vintage broadcast, 16mm field reports

* Section 5 — The Cost (10% of runtime) = slowest, heaviest pacing of the entire video

  * Average shot length: 8-12 seconds
  * Show who was harmed: archival photographs of victims, court statements, families
  * Music drops to near-silent
  * Long holds on photographs of named individuals
  * Voice slows by an additional -3% rate in this section
  * Letterbox tightens slightly — vignette darkens — the frame literally narrows around the cost

* Section 6 — The Cover-Up (10% of runtime) = clinical, procedural

  * Average shot length: 5-7 seconds
  * Document destruction, classification, denial under oath, settlements with non-disclosure
  * Use the shredder mograph here
  * Tone: methodical, not outraged — describe the cover-up as a procedure

* Section 7 — The Disclosure (10% of runtime) = subtle relief, never triumph

  * Average shot length: 6-9 seconds
  * Whose work brought this to light: journalist, congressional committee, FOIA requester, whistleblower
  * Name them. Hold on their photograph. Cite the year of disclosure.
  * Music: single low note holds, slowly rising in volume

* Section 8 — The Aftermath / The Lesson (5% of runtime) = final restraint

  * Average shot length: 10-15 seconds
  * Who was held accountable. Who wasn't.
  * Single closing observation, on a near-black screen, in typewriter font
  * Hold the final frame for 4-5 seconds in total silence

* Avoid throughout:

  * Any pacing that suggests excitement about the revelations
  * Quick cuts to manufacture urgency
  * Music swells that telegraph what the viewer should feel
  * Cutting away from a document before the viewer has had time to read it
  * Over-stylized motion design — this is documentary, not music video
  * Any sequence longer than 90 seconds without a document on screen — the documents are the protagonist of every episode
editing.edit_text_rules: Typewriter font reveals only. Maximum 7 words per text event. Words appear letter by letter with audible keystroke. Three categories of on-screen text: 1) Document quotes (white text on black background, hold 4 seconds), 2) Names and dates (typewriter font with date in parenthesis, hold 3 seconds), 3) Final closing observation (single line, white on near-black, hold 5 seconds before fade). Never use bold or italic to manufacture emphasis — let the words carry weight on their own.
editing.edit_end: Last 30 seconds: near-black screen. The closing observation appears in typewriter font, one letter at a time. Hold for 5 seconds after fully typed. Cut to total black. Hold black for 3 seconds in silence. Then a single line: 'Primary sources for this episode in description.' Hold 3 seconds. End.
editing.edit_special: There is no celebrity face. The document is the protagonist of every shot. Every section transition is marked by a moment of total silence — 2 seconds — followed by the next section's first sentence. Section title cards appear in typewriter font, lower-left corner, in this format: '§ III. THE DOCUMENTS'. Section titles do not announce themselves loudly — they appear and fade. The narrator never refers to themselves, never says 'we', never asks the viewer a question — they read the case file. The viewer is alone with the evidence.

narrator.persona: Investigative journalist reading a case file. Mid-40s. Has read this material twice. Knows what is coming next but reads it as if presenting it to a jury for the first time. Voice carries the weight of the evidence without performing emotion about it. Imagine the narrator of The Fog of War or a senior Frontline correspondent.
narrator.doc_structure: Reads section titles aloud only for the major divisions. Reads document quotes in a slightly flatter, more careful register. Pauses 1.2 seconds at section breaks, 2 seconds before a document quote, 0.6 seconds between sentences.

shorts.formula:
FUNDAMENTAL DIFFERENCES FROM LONG-FORM

ELEMENT	LONG-FORM	SHORTS
Aspect ratio	16:9 horizontal	9:16 vertical
Duration	12–18 minutes	40–60 seconds
Hook time	20 seconds (cold quote)	0 seconds — single document, single quote
Intro sequence	Section structure	No sections — single revelation
Document face time	30-40% of runtime	60-70% of runtime — the document IS the frame
Music volume	Mostly silent	Still mostly silent — typewriter and room tone instead
Captions	Document-style typewriter	Centre frame, typewriter font, word by word
Letterboxing	Always on (2.39:1)	Never — full vertical frame
Film grain	14-18 amount	10-14 amount
Colour grade	Master grade applied	Master grade, untouched

SHORTS — STRUCTURE
Every REDACTED Short follows a precise 5-part structure. No deviations.

▸ SECONDS 0–4: The document fills the frame. Single quoted sentence from the document is read in a flat voice. Document is real, declassified, visible. No music. Room tone only.
▸ SECONDS 4–10: Pull back from the document. Operation name appears in typewriter font with year. Voice reads: '[Operation Name]. [Year].' Then 1 second of silence.
▸ SECONDS 10–35: The revelation — the single specific fact the document proves. Two or three sentences maximum. Cut between document close-ups, a single archival photograph, and the timeline mograph. Subtle piano enters very quietly.
▸ SECONDS 35–50: The cost or the cover-up — one specific harmed person named, or one specific destroyed document named. Hold on a single image for the entire window.
▸ SECONDS 50–60: Single closing line in typewriter font on black. Total silence. The line is a fact, not a question. Fade.

SHORTS — CAPTION STYLE
Captions are typewriter-style throughout. They are not modern social-media captions.
Position: Centred horizontally. Vertically positioned in the lower third — roughly 70% down the frame.
Font: Courier or IBM Selectric — always monospace, always all caps.
Size: Each word approximately 7-8% of frame width. Smaller than Dopamine Loop shorts on purpose — these are case-file captions, not social-media stoppers.
Animation: Letter by letter, typewriter speed. No fade — letters appear instantly with keystroke sound.
Background: No background box. Subtle text shadow only (1-2px, 30% black).
Colour treatment: White text by default. Red only on the word REDACTED or the word CLASSIFIED. Never yellow.

SHORTS — SOUND DESIGN
Music level: -16dB to -14dB under voice. Quieter than most channels' shorts on purpose — these are not designed to compete with energy, they are designed to feel like a leaked clip from a documentary.
Transition sounds: Document rustle (-12dB) on cuts between document close-ups. Typewriter keystroke (-8dB) on every text reveal. Stamp impact (-6dB) on classification graphic.
Bass boost: Slight (+2dB below 80Hz) — enough to feel weight on phone speakers, not enough to feel like a trap drop.
Voice treatment: Slight room reverb (-22dB return). Voice should sound like it is being recorded in an enclosed room, not a studio.
Silence: The final 4 seconds before fade are total silence. The classification stamp at second 9 has 1 second of total silence after it. Silence is the channel's signature sound.
shorts.hook:
Shorts Hook Rule

The first 1-2 seconds must show ONE real declassified document with ONE highlighted sentence
The voice reads that sentence in a flat, measured tone
The sentence must be so specific that the viewer cannot scroll without knowing the rest
Use only documents that exist — verifiable, citable, real
Never invent quotes
Never use lorem ipsum or placeholder text
Never fake a letterhead
Hook formulas:
'A 1955 internal Agency memo authorized testing on subjects who were not informed.'
'In the deposition, the executive testified under oath that they knew in 1979.'
'The Pentagon Papers contain 7,000 pages. This is what is on page 432.'
'Of the 47 names in the redacted document, three are now on corporate boards.'
The first frame should be the document itself, not a face
The first sound should be the voice and the typewriter — no music, no impact
shorts.visuals:
Shorts Visual Style

Document-first throughout — the page is the protagonist
Slow Ken-Burns zoom on quoted paragraphs
Yellow underline highlight (subtle, not neon) on the line being read
Cut between: the document, a single archival photograph, a stamp coming down, a typewriter typing the next line
No modern stock footage
No green-text terminals, no hooded figures, no spinning newspapers
Film grain at 10-14 amount — slightly cleaner than long-form to read well on phone
Letterboxing OFF for vertical — use the full frame
Text overlays in typewriter font, centred lower-third, word by word with keystroke sound
Classification stamps used sparingly — once per short maximum, at the operation reveal moment
Final frame: closing observation in typewriter font on near-black, hold 3 seconds, fade to total black
Shorts should feel like a leaked 60-second cut from a documentary, not like a social-media reaction
shorts.music: Sparse minimalist piano enters at second 10, runs at -16dB under voice, drops to silence at second 50, returns only as a single sustained low note in the final 2 seconds before fade. No trending audio — REDACTED does not chase trends, it builds an aesthetic the algorithm learns to identify.

thumbnail.thumb_concept:
Thumbnail Concept Rule

Every thumbnail is composed like a case-file evidence card photographed under desk lamp light
Use one of three composition types:
  1. The Document — a single declassified page tilted slightly on a dark wood or manila surface, with one line highlighted in yellow underline, redaction bar visible elsewhere
  2. The Stamp — close-up of a real classification stamp (TOP SECRET, EYES ONLY, CONFIDENTIAL) on an aged document with visible paper grain
  3. The Photograph — a single archival photograph (always real, always sourced) with a documented caption in typewriter font, slightly off-centre, with a redaction bar over one element
Lighting: always single warm desk-lamp source from upper left, deep shadow from upper right
Texture: paper grain, faint coffee ring, slight crease, edge wear — every thumbnail should look like it has been handled
The channel name REDACTED appears small in lower right, in typewriter font, in red
Never a face filling the frame
Never exaggerated expressions
Never modern composition tricks (arrows, circles, neon outlines)
Thumbnails should look like archive material, not YouTube material — and over time, viewers will learn to recognise the format instantly
thumbnail.thumb_text_formula:
Text Font Style on Thumbnail

Typewriter font only — Courier, IBM Selectric, or American Typewriter
All caps for the operation name, mixed case for the descriptor line
Colour: bone white on dark, federal-record red ONLY on the word REDACTED or CLASSIFIED
Add subtle ink-press texture so the text feels stamped rather than printed
No glow, no drop shadow beyond a 1px hard shadow
Text size: large enough to read on mobile but not dominating — the document is the protagonist
Maximum 4 words plus a date
Format: '[OPERATION NAME] / [YEAR]' or '[YEAR] / [SPECIFIC FACT]'
thumbnail.thumb_title_formula:
Title Formula

The CIA program they renamed twice
What the 1955 memo actually authorized
The Pentagon Papers page they tried to bury
The deposition that ended a billion-dollar industry
The FOIA request that took 28 years
The Church Committee finding nobody discusses
The internal memo that contradicts the public statement
What the 1977 ExxonMobil report concluded
The Tuskegee study did not end when they said it ended
Five lines of testimony that changed everything
The document marked DESTROY that survived
The whistleblower the agency tried to discredit
What the deposition transcript actually says
The operation that ran for 20 years before it was named
The settlement that bought silence
The internal memo predicted everything
The leaked cable nobody published
The hearing that vanished from public record
Three paragraphs that changed the case
The page they redacted said this
thumbnail.thumb_forbidden:
Forbidden Thumbnail Elements
No Anonymous masks
No hooded hackers
No green text terminals
No Illuminati symbols, pyramids, or all-seeing eyes
No lizard imagery or reptilian framing
No current-events political figures unless they are direct subjects of declassified material
No conspiracy-board red string aesthetic
No melodramatic emoji or arrows
No blood splatter
No screaming faces
No neon glow
No cliché 'WAKE UP' typography
No fake leaked watermarks
No lorem ipsum or placeholder document text — real documents only
No more than 4-5 words plus a date
No bright yellow tabloid palette
No motion-blurred 'urgent breaking' aesthetic
No countdown timers
No stock photos of generic agents in suits
No dramatic close-up of a single eye
No whistleblower clichés (hooded figure with red lighting)
No overlay text that promises more than the documents prove

seo.description:
SEO Package Template Rules

SEO title should be evidentiary and specific — name the operation, name the year, name what the documents prove
Main keyword should appear in the first 5 words of the title
Description opens with one sentence stating which documents the episode is built on, with declassification year
Link to primary sources in description — FOIA references, congressional record citations, court docket numbers when available
Use 5-10 hashtags
Use a mix of investigative-journalism tags and specific operation tags
Include tags related to:
  declassified history
  FOIA documents
  congressional hearings
  intelligence operations
  corporate cover-ups
  whistleblower testimony
  investigative journalism
  primary source history
Titles should be under 70 characters when possible
Descriptions should be evidentiary, not breathless
CTA should invite the viewer to read the primary sources themselves
seo.tags: declassified, FOIA, primary source, investigative journalism, intelligence history, cold war declassified, congressional hearing, MKUltra, COINTELPRO, Iran-Contra, Pentagon Papers, Snowden, whistleblower, archive, document, deposition, testimony, hearing, Church Committee, Pike Committee, CIA, NSA, FBI, declassified documents, leaked memos, corporate coverup
seo.cta: Description CTA: 'Primary sources used in this episode are linked below. Read the documents yourself.' / 'If you want to support archival work like this, subscribe.' / 'Comment with operations or documented cases you want examined.' / 'Subscribe for one investigation per week, built on the record.'
